# Visit Auschwitz - Full Content Reference

> Comprehensive visitor guide for the Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial and Museum.
> Author: Łukasz, licensed Auschwitz guide since 2006.

Website: https://www.visitauschwitz.info
Languages: en, pl

## Discover Auschwitz Visiting Guidelines & Extra Info

URL: https://www.visitauschwitz.info/en/supplement
Description: Up-to-date reliable information on Auschwitz tours, including important regulations, visit conditions, and helpful visitor details.

### FAQ

**Q: Should I Go with a Guide or Visit Auschwitz on My Own?**
A: Independent Visit: Allows more time for reflection and exploration beyond the standard route. However, it requires thorough preparation to grasp the immense historical significance of the site. Guided tours are mandatory at certain hours and for groups over 10. Read the reviews before you decide. Gu

**Q: What is the Best Time to Visit Auschwitz and How to Skip the Queues?**
A: Fewer Crowds: The site is quieter from November to March. Visit at 8 a.m. or late in the day for fewer crowds, as mornings and early afternoons are typically crowded with large groups. Skip the Queues: Individual visitors and groups of up to 15 people can bypass the long entrance queues (ask the sta

**Q: Where is Auschwitz Museum Located and How to Get There?**
A: 📍 Location: Auschwitz is in Oświęcim – 70 km / 43 mi from Kraków (~1 hour by train or ~1.5 hours by bus) 🚆 By Train: Oświęcim station is ~1.5 km from the Memorial 🚌 By Bus: Lajkonik buses go directly to the Museum entrance 🚗 By Car: Take the A4 or DK44 route 🚐 By Private Transfer: Confirm the f

**Q: Which Exhibits and Sites Can I See at the Former Camp?**
A: The Tour Page outlines the sites visited during a standard sightseeing and beyond (use the button in the bottom right to find them on the map). Most visitors explore two locations: 1. Auschwitz I Main Camp ↓ Main gate "Arbeit macht frei" ↓ Blocks 4 & 5 – Exhibits on mass extermination and confiscate

**Q:  How to Prepare for a Smooth Auschwitz Visit?**
A: Since Auschwitz is not a typical tourist site, it's essential to be prepared both logistically and emotionally. When planning your visit, consider the conditions outlined above and your physical limitations, and be sure to check these pages: • Tickets – Learn about tour types and booking options • A

**Q: Can I Eat or Drink Inside Auschwitz Museum?**
A: Eating is prohibited inside the Museum grounds, but snacks can be consumed outside. Staying hydrated is encouraged —water and non-alcoholic drinks are allowed. Restrooms are available at the entrances, with only a few facilities inside the Memorial: 🚾 Toilets at the Exhibition

**Q: What Should I Bring for My Auschwitz Tour?**
A: ✅ Required: Entry pass (printed or digital) & valid ID ✅ Recommended: Water bottle, an umbrella in case of rain or sun, and trekking shoes due to uneven terrain. The sightseeing is mostly outdoors, so dress for the season. ❌ Keep personal belongings minimal due to baggage size restrictions, avoiding

**Q: Which Items Are Prohibited Inside the Museum?**
A: To enter the Memorial , you must not carry any of the following: large bags, inappropriate clothing, loud audio devices, tripods, drones, pets, alcohol, drugs, weapons (including knives and scissors), hazardous substances, and any political, religious, or nationalistic flags, banners, or symbols (ex

**Q: Can I Take Photos or Record Videos at Auschwitz Camp?**
A: Personal photography and filming are allowed except: 🚫 Indoors with flash 🚫 Recording guided lectures 🚫 The victims' hair exhibit (block 4) 🚫 Block 11 basements 🚫 Security checkpoints

**Q: Are Children Allowed on Auschwitz Tours?**
A: It is allowed to visit with children, but it's not recommended for those under 14 due to the sensitive content. Parents should consider their child's emotional maturity. Visitors with infants should use a baby carrier as strollers are not allowed inside buildings. On the ground floor of the receptio

**Q: Is Auschwitz Accessible for Visitors with Disabilities?**
A: The Museum is not fully adapted to maintain the site's historical authenticity. Visitors with a disability ID and a guardian may receive special entry. Hearing aid devices may not work well with the headset. Wheelchairs are available on site, but they cannot be used in the Auschwitz I Main Camp buil

- Arranging Auschwitz Sightseeing Discover essential visitor preparation tips, answers to common questions, and practical advice to help you make the most of your time at the Auschwitz Memorial.

- Contribute to My Efforts
- Works of Auschwitz Survivors
- Wincenty Gawron Wincenty Gawron, born in 1908 in Stara Wieś, studied art in Lwów, Kraków, and Warszawa. After World War II began, he joined a secret military group but was arrested in 1941 and sent to
- Dinah Gottliebova
- Mieczysław Kościelniak Mieczysław Kościelniak, born in 1912 in Kalisz, was a well-known Polish artist. In 1941, he was arrested and sent to Auschwitz (camp number 15261).
- Władysław Siwek Born in 1907 in Niepołomice, Władysław Siwek worked for the State Railways while studying at the Academy of Fine Arts in Kraków. Arrested in 1940, he was placed in Montelupich Prison a
- Janina Tollik Janina Tollik was born in 1910 in Janów. She studied at the Academy of Fine Arts in Kraków from 1931 to 1935. In 1941, her resistance work led to her arrest, and in 1942, she was registe
- Sources Attribution Empowered by open-source collaboration—bringing Auschwitz navigation to life with community-driven tools.

## Auschwitz Routes: Main Sites & Hidden Spots with Map

URL: https://www.visitauschwitz.info/en/tour
Description: Navigate Auschwitz Memorial using detailed routes, photos of key sites, and an interactive map designed to guide your visit.

### FAQ

**Q: ⚙️ Does the Auschwitz Tour Route Ever Change?**
A: The 3.5-hour tour includes a visit to either block 6 or 7 in Auschwitz and either brick or wooden barracks in Birkenau. Specific location requests can be made. Private tours may start at Birkenau if there are long entry lines at Auschwitz. Certain tours offer access to locations beyond the standard 

**Q: ☔ What Are the Auschwitz Tour Conditions and Regulations?**
A: The tour involves several hours of walking on uneven, rocky, and muddy terrain, with no weather protection. Learn more about tour conditions. Eating and smoking are not allowed. Please silence your phone and refrain from using flash indoors. Learn more about tour regulations.

**Q: 🚾 Where Are Toilets and Places to Rest on the Auschwitz Grounds?**
A: At Auschwitz, restrooms are in block 18, next to block 7. Some buildings, like blocks 17 and 27, have benches and air conditioning to cool down on hot days. At Birkenau, restrooms are located to the right behind the gate. Another restroom and benches can be found in the wooded area to the right behi

### Sections

- Auschwitz Route Overview The 50 locations are listed in the order they appear on a typical tour. Use the button in the bottom right to find them on the Auschwitz camp map (or tour virtually ). The tou
- Places to Visit at Auschwitz
- Auschwitz I Main Camp
- Block 24
- Preserved Blocks 2 and 3
- The Camp Heaters
- Room 6 – Canada
- Block 5 – Material Evidence of Crime Luggage confiscated from deportees, including eyeglasses, Jewish prayer shawls, items of people with disabilities, metal pots, as well as shoes and suitcases on th
- Swimming Pool
- Block 7 – Living Conditions
- Block 10 – Experiments
- Torture Hooks
- Block 11 – The Death Block On the ground floor, you see the summary court where Poles were sentenced to death in brief trials before being executed at the Death Wall; in the basement, you visit the in
- Block 11 – Resistance
- Block 27 – Israel
- Block 20 – France
- Road Rollers
- Block 18 – Hungary
- Block 17 – Austria
- Block 16 – Slovakia
- Block 16 – Czech Republic (upstairs)
- Block 15 – Poland
- Block 14 – Russia (currently unavailable)
- Block 13 – Roma
- Inner Fence
- Roll Call Square
- Auschwitz Exit
- SS Accommodation
- The Mass Grave
- Auschwitz II-Birkenau
- Birkenau Selection Site
- Registration Offices
- Camp Kitchens
- Fire Reservoirs
- Crematoriums and Gas Chambers Ruins
- Royal Ditch
- Canada
- Ponds with Ashes
- Mexico and BII Sectors
- Wooden Barracks
- Brick Barracks

## Auschwitz Entry Guidelines: Rules, Facilities & Tips

URL: https://www.visitauschwitz.info/en/museum
Description: Discover what to expect at the Memorial, including Auschwitz map, visitor guidelines, site rules, and tips for a meaningful visit.

### FAQ

**Q: 🕒 What Are the Opening Hours of the Auschwitz Memorial?**
A: Entrance hours depend on the month and whether you visit with or without a guide. The Memorial is closed on January 1, December 25, Easter Sunday, and occasionally for events.

### Sections

- Auschwitz Visitor Service Center I took this photo at the entrance to Auschwitz. It's the first thing you'll see when you arrive. Open the Auschwitz map with the button in the bottom right.
- Auschwitz Memorial Vicinity Stay longer to discover the area or relax in nature.
- The City of Oświęcim
- Castle Museum
- Town Hall Museum
- International Youth Meeting Centre
- Nature Around Auschwitz

## Auschwitz Directions: Find the Best Way to Get There

URL: https://www.visitauschwitz.info/en/arrival
Description: Get to Auschwitz easily with complete transportation info, including parking and routes from Kraków.

### FAQ

**Q: How to Get to Auschwitz From Kraków?**
A: Reach Oświęcim in just 1 hour for 15 PLN by train. Reach the Memorial directly in 1.5 hours by car or bus. Get around Kraków using jakdojade.pl

**Q: How to Get Around the Memorial and the City?**
A: The train station is well connected to the Memorial and city center. Use local taxis and public transport to get around the area.

**Q: What Private Transport Options Are Available?**
A: Cab drivers and private transport companies can take you to Auschwitz from almost anywhere. Determine the exact price before your trip and let the service provider know that they can leave contact information here.

**Q: What Should I Know When Planning My Visit?**
A: Don't travel right before a tour that is physically demanding in itself. Find accommodations nearby and visit Auschwitz after a good night's sleep. Remember that you are visiting two parts of the former camp in different locations shown below.

### Sections

- How to Get to Birkenau
- How to Get to Auschwitz by Car Click the map icon on the right and select “Parking lots” in the upper right corner to see the nearest parking options. The links below show the locations on Google Maps
- Parking Lots near Birkenau
- Direct Bus Kraków → Auschwitz
- How to Get to Auschwitz by Train
- Airports near Auschwitz

## Auschwitz Tickets: Discover Tour Types & Booking Tips

URL: https://www.visitauschwitz.info/en/tickets
Description: How to get tickets to Auschwitz, including booking options, pricing details and tips for visitors from a licensed guide.

### FAQ

**Q: How to Visit Auschwitz When Entry Passes Are Gone?**
A: If local companies are fully booked and no more passes are available online, there’s still one last option — though it comes with risk. Take the first train or bus (check how to arrive ), or find accommodation nearby, and be there by 7 a.m. or earlier to try to get a ticket on site (details under th

**Q: What to Do If Your Auschwitz Tour Was Canceled?**
A: Cancellations typically happen when the booking wasn’t made directly with the Museum. Your first step should be to check the official system at visit.auschwitz.org. If no tours are available, try local tour providers — though prices may be high, and availability limited. If tickets are completely so

**Q: Which Documents Are Required to Enter the Muzeum?**
A: ✔ A valid photo ID (passport, driver's license, etc.) is required and must be shown before scanning your entry pass at the Auschwitz Memorial entrance. ✔ Your entry pass (printed or digital with a visible barcode) is scanned before security and again at the reception area.

**Q: How to Book Your Free Auschwitz Entry Pass?**
A: To visit Auschwitz on your own, you must reserve an individual entry pass online, at least 7 days in advance. Go to visit.auschwitz.org and choose these options: Visit for individuals -> select a date -> Tour for individuals without an educator These afternoon slots are listed without any language a

**Q: What to Do on the Day of Your Tour?**
A: For directions check the Arrival page and once there you follow these steps: 1. You can enter 30 minutes early ( skipping the long line ) 2. Present your ID and scan the entry pass 3. Walk through security gates (metal detection) 4. Scan your ticket again to go downstairs 6. Get outside of the tunne

**Q: How to Visit Auschwitz on Your Own?**
A: Outdoors, use our map to find your location. Indoors, follow a guidebook and move in the same direction as the guided groups. Auschwitz self-guided tour tips: Be sure to review the preparation guide. Without a historical background, the place may not give you much, so consider a guided tour first.

**Q: How to Book a Guided Tour for Individual Visitors?**
A: Online - Safe Go to visit.auschwitz.org and choose these options: Visit for individuals -> select a date -> select language -> select the visiting hour -> add to basket On Site - Risky Each day, a limited number of entry spots become available on site. Arriving early in the morning gives you a chanc

**Q: Which Languages Are Available and Is There an Audio Guide?**
A: Guided tours for individual visitors are available in Czech, English, French, German, Italian, Polish, Russian, Slovak, and Spanish. There is no audioguide system at Auschwitz Museum, but you will use a headset to hear your guide clearly during the tour.

**Q: What Are the Ticket Prices for Joining an Auschwitz Guided Tour?**
A: 130 PLN (120* PLN) 3.5-h regular tour (for groups over 6 people a private tour is cheaper: 7*130>870) 170 PLN (160* PLN) 6-h study tour 60 PLN 2.5-h online tour *The discount is available to students under 26, people over 75 or with a disability card. In Polish the sightseeing is 10-20 PLN cheaper a

**Q: When to Arrive and What to Do on the Day of Your Visit?**
A: Get there at least half an hour before your tour. For directions check the Arrival page and once there you follow these steps: 1. Enter the visitor service center ( skipping the long line ) 2. Present your ID and entry pass (f you lose your ticket, ask the staff for assistance with reprinting it on 

**Q: How and When Can I Book a Private Auschwitz Guided Tour?**
A: Once on visit.auschwitz.org select the 'Visit for groups' option. After registering and submitting your request, you’ll receive a response by email. It’s best to book at least one month in advance. Group bookings are available up to 5 days before the visit, or up to 2 days in advance for an addition

**Q: How Much Does It Cost to Hire a Guide for an Auschwitz Tour?**
A: The prices* for groups of up to 30 / 20 / 10 people: 1020 / 970 / 870 PLN 2.5- / 3.5-h general tour It may cost PLN 34 per person = 1020/30 1290 / 1240 / 1140 PLN 6-h study tour 1800 / 1750 / 1650 PLN 8-h study tour 690 PLN 2.5-h online tour Open the official price list pdf document valid from 1.05.

**Q: Which Languages Are Offered for the Private Sightseeing?**
A: Private guided tours are available in: Croatian, Czech, Dutch, English, French, German, Greek, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Polish, Russian, Serbian, Slovak, Spanish, Swedish, Ukrainian.

**Q: When to Arrive and What to Do on Site?**
A: 1. Arrive at least 40 minutes early so that you can use restrooms, in the meantime the group leader should check in at the front desk (see photo below) to meet your guide 2. Walk through security gates with your guide 3. Everyone presents their ID and entry passes (distribute tickets before arrival 

**Q: Why Can Auschwitz Tour Bookings Be Canceled?**
A: Museum guides can be booked up to 90 days in advance, while tour companies may charge at any time. Sometimes, guides are requested only after the entry fee is paid, which might not be possible. You may also be replaced at the last minute by someone willing to pay more for your spot, causing the tour

**Q: Are There Cheaper Auschwitz Tours and What Are the Risks?**
A: Prices for organized Auschwitz tours usually range from 160 to 300 PLN per person. Booking in advance is cheaper, and can be as low as 40 PLN for special offers but these tours are often cancelled as explained above. Scammers try to sell free tickets or fail to explain that you are only paying for t

**Q: What Causes Crowds at the Auschwitz Memorial?**
A: Combining Auschwitz tour with a visit to Wieliczka Salt Mine, many companies arrive at the same time. Hence the long lines and crowds at the exhibition in the morning and early afternoon.

**Q: How Tiring Can a Visit to Auschwitz Be?**
A: Companies try to gather as large a group as possible, so you usually spend half a day surrounded by 30 strangers. Imagine being picked up in jammed Kraków, a long drive, queues, and rushing around. Moreover, visiting Auschwitz and the Salt Mine is physically demanding, so it’s not advisable to combi

**Q: Is Visiting Auschwitz With a Big Group a Waste of Time?**
A: The 30 people who see the place for the first time have to gather several times. During the breaks, some try to use the crowded restrooms or eat something in the crowded restaurant. What the guide says often needs to be translated, which reduces the available time for narration even by half.

**Q: How Can a Tight Schedule Affect Your Auschwitz Visit?**
A: A busy schedule can make you feel rushed. Fixed itinerary only allows you to visit certain places, missing out on so much. It is much better to book directly, go there yourself and visit the places you want at your own pace.

### Sections

- How to Get Auschwitz Tickets Auschwitz tickets are limited in number — booking in advance on the official website is the best way to secure your entry.
- Visit Auschwitz Without a Guide A maximum of ten people can enter the Auschwitz Museum without a guide, but only in the afternoon. Entrance is free, but a pass is still required.
- Guided Tours of Auschwitz Most visitors choose organized tours for their convenience. However, to avoid last-minute cancellations, it's best to book a tour arranged by the Museum through visit.auschwi
- Join Auschwitz Guided Tour Join a group of up to 30 people on a 3.5- or 6-hour tour with a fixed itinerary. For a better experience, booking a private tour is recommended. It offers a wider selection 
- Private Auschwitz Guided Tour If you hire a guide you can choose which places to visit and what to explain in detail. You can start 30 min early, but you lose time if late.
- Organized Tour of Auschwitz Auschwitz tours are available through hotel receptions and apps, but intermediaries often add a commission, and bookings can be risky. To avoid extra costs and ensure your 
- Verified Local Suppliers of Auschwitz Tours
- Disadvantages of Organized Tours Some companies (other than those listed above) cancel bookings at the last minute or change departure times, sometimes picking people up at night so they have to wait 

## Contact Us: Auschwitz Memorial Visitor Information

URL: https://www.visitauschwitz.info/en/contact
Description: Reach out for support with tickets, tours, or general visitor info. A response will be sent within a few days.

### Auschwitz Guide Turned Developer to Help Visitors Prepare

URL: https://www.visitauschwitz.info/en/posts/auschwitz-guide-turned-developer-to-help-visitors-prepare
Description: For years, I guided visitors through Auschwitz and answered the same questions. That’s why I created a website to help people prepare for their visit.
Published: 2025-10-12

---

## Key Facts

- Location: Oświęcim, Poland (70 km from Kraków)
- Address: Więźniów Oświęcimia 55, 32-600 Oświęcim
- Official museum: https://www.auschwitz.org/
- Official booking: https://visit.auschwitz.org/
- Two sites: Auschwitz I (Main Camp) and Auschwitz II-Birkenau (3 km apart)
- UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1979
- ~2 million visitors per year
